title: Kottayam district

infobox.value: **Revenue Divisions: 2   * [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") * [Pala](/wiki/Pala,_Kerala "Pala, Kerala")  Taluks: 5   * [Kottayam](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_taluk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kottayam taluk (page does not exist)") * [Vaikom](/w/index.php?title=Vaikom_taluk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vaikom taluk (page does not exist)") * [Meenachil](/w/index.php?title=Meenachil_taluk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Meenachil taluk (page does not exist)") * [Changanassery](/w/index.php?title=Changanassery_taluk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Changanassery taluk (page does not exist)") * [Kanjirappally](/w/index.php?title=Kanjirappally_taluk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kanjirappally taluk (page does not exist)")**

[Kumarakom](/wiki/Kumarakom "Kumarakom"), [Malarikkal](/wiki/Malarikkal "Malarikkal"), [Nair Service Society](/wiki/Nair_Service_Society "Nair Service Society") at [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [SB College](/wiki/SB_College "SB College") [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [CMS College Kottayam](/wiki/CMS_College_Kottayam "CMS College Kottayam"), and [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom") Boat Jetty. | |

(IPA: [[koːʈːɐjɐm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q36236_(mal)-Vis_M-%E0%B4%95%E0%B5%8B%E0%B4%9F%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%AF%E0%B4%82.wav "File:LL-Q36236 (mal)-Vis M-കോട്ടയം.wav")) is one of [14 districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala") in the [Indian state](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"). Kottayam district comprises six municipal towns: [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [Pala](/wiki/Pala,_Kerala "Pala, Kerala"), [Erattupetta](/wiki/Erattupetta "Erattupetta"), [Ettumanoor](/wiki/Ettumanoor "Ettumanoor"), and [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom"). Situated in the central part of Kerala, Kottayam shares its borders with Ernakulam, Idukki, Pathanamthitta, and Alappuzha districts. It is the only [district](/wiki/List_of_districts_in_India "List of districts in India") in Kerala that does not border either the Arabian Sea or another Indian state.

This article is about the district in Kerala, India. For its name sake city, see [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"). **Kottayam district** (IPA: [[koːʈːɐjɐm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q36236_(mal)-Vis_M-%E0%B4%95%E0%B5%8B%E0%B4%9F%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%AF%E0%B4%82.wav "File:LL-Q36236 (mal)-Vis M-കോട്ടയം.wav")) is one of [14 districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala") in the [Indian state](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"). Kottayam district comprises six municipal towns: [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [Pala](/wiki/Pala,_Kerala "Pala, Kerala"), [Erattupetta](/wiki/Erattupetta "Erattupetta"), [Ettumanoor](/wiki/Ettumanoor "Ettumanoor"), and [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom"). Situated in the central part of Kerala, Kottayam shares its borders with Ernakulam, Idukki, Pathanamthitta, and Alappuzha districts. It is the only [district](/wiki/List_of_districts_in_India "List of districts in India") in Kerala that does not border either the Arabian Sea or another Indian state.

The district is bordered by hills in the east, and the [Vembanad Lake](/wiki/Vembanad_Lake "Vembanad Lake") and [paddy fields](/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field") of [Kuttanad](/wiki/Kuttanad "Kuttanad") on the west. The area's geographic features include paddy fields, highlands, and hills. As of the 2011 census, 28.6% of the district's residents live in urban areas, and it reports a 97.2% [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_in_India "Literacy in India").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 2008, the district became the first tobacco-free district in India.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Kottayam registered the lowest [Multidimensional Poverty Index](/wiki/Multidimensional_Poverty_Index "Multidimensional Poverty Index") (MPI) of zero among all districts of India, indicating no deprivation as per the report published by [Oxford Poverty and Human Development Initiative](/wiki/Oxford_Poverty_and_Human_Development_Initiative "Oxford Poverty and Human Development Initiative") and [UNDP](/wiki/UNDP "UNDP") for districts across India.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The district's headquarters are based in the city of Kottayam.

[Hindustan Newsprint Limited](/wiki/Hindustan_Newsprint_Limited "Hindustan Newsprint Limited") and [Rubber Board](/wiki/Rubber_Board "Rubber Board") are two central government organizations located in the district. The headquarters of two religious communities in Kerala are also in the Kottayam District: [Nair Service Society](/wiki/Nair_Service_Society "Nair Service Society") and the [Indian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Indian_Orthodox_Church "Indian Orthodox Church").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name Kottayam is a combination of the words "kotta" and "akam" in the local language of [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), meaning "interior of a fort".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of Kerala](/wiki/History_of_Kerala "History of Kerala")

### Prehistoric period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistoric period")]

Main article: [Prehistory](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory")

A substantial portion of Kottayam district may have been under the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") during prehistoric times. Marine fossils have been found in an area near [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), thus supporting the hypothesis.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

However, there are archaeological evidences of the early human inhabitation period of this district, including ancient fossils, stone inscriptions and monuments, in the archeological sites like the excavation sites, the caves, the temples, etc. The literary works of the [Sangam period](/wiki/Sangam_period "Sangam period") also help to take a look into the ancient period of the district.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

### Classical Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classical Antiquity")]

Main article: [Classical Antiquity](/wiki/Classical_Antiquity "Classical Antiquity")

#### Chera dynasty and the Keralaputras of ancient Tamilakam (up to 5th century CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Chera dynasty and the Keralaputras of ancient Tamilakam (up to 5th century CE)")]

Main article: [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty")

See also: [Tamilakam](/wiki/Tamilakam "Tamilakam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Chera_country_%28early_historic_south_India%29.jpg/250px-Chera_country_%28early_historic_south_India%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chera_country_(early_historic_south_India).jpg)

The extent of the [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty") (known as *Keralaputras* in the inscriptions of the emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka") of the [Maurya Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") (322 [BCE](/wiki/Before_Common_Era "Before Common Era") – 184 BCE)[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) during the early centuries of the [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").

Early members of the [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty") (first few centuries of the [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era")) had their original headquarters in a region called *Kuzhamur* at *Kuttanad* in the ancient [Tamilakam](/wiki/Tamilakam "Tamilakam") and were sometimes known as the *Kuttuvans*.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty") is mentioned as *Keralaputras* in the inscriptions of the emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka") of the [Maurya Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") (322 [BCE](/wiki/Before_Common_Era "Before Common Era") – 184 BCE).[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The province *Kuttanad* of the ancient kingdom of *Keralaputras* included the modern-day districts of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), [Ernakulam](/wiki/Ernakulam_district "Ernakulam district"), [Idukki](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district"), and parts of [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha_district "Alappuzha district"), which can be defined as the region between the rivers [Periyar](/wiki/Periyar_river "Periyar river") and [Pamba](/wiki/Pamba_River "Pamba River").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) It was bounded by the [Ay kingdom](/wiki/Ay_kingdom "Ay kingdom") to the south, which included the regions between [Pamba River](/wiki/Pamba_River "Pamba River") and [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari") (Cape Comorin), and the province of *Kudanad*, which lies between the rivers [Periyar](/wiki/Periyar_river "Periyar river") and [Chaliyar](/wiki/Chaliyar_river "Chaliyar river") (modern-day districts of [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur_district "Thrissur district"), [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad_district "Palakkad district"), and [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district")), to the north.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

It was during the reign of [Uthiyan Cheralathan](/wiki/Uthiyan_Cheralathan "Uthiyan Cheralathan") (105–130 CE) that the [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty") began to expand towards the northern and the eastern regions of *Kuttanad* by conquering the provinces of *Kudanadu* and *[Kongu Nadu](/wiki/Kongu_Nadu "Kongu Nadu")*.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Afterwards the dynasty got split into three branches and fixed their capitals at [Muziris](/wiki/Muziris "Muziris"), [Tyndis](/wiki/Tyndis "Tyndis"), and [Caroura](/wiki/Karur "Karur"), respectively, as seen in the ancient [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world "Greco-Roman world") travelogues as well as the [Old Tamil](/wiki/Old_Tamil "Old Tamil") literary works of the [Sangam period](/wiki/Sangam_period "Sangam period").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) During the reign of *Narmudi Cheral*, the regions included in the [Kingdom of Ezhimala](/wiki/Mushika_dynasty "Mushika dynasty") was also added to the Chera empire.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

#### Invasions and social transformation from 6th century CE to 800 CE

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Invasions and social transformation from 6th century CE to 800 CE")]

See also: [Kalabhra dynasty](/wiki/Kalabhra_dynasty "Kalabhra dynasty"), [Pallava dynasty](/wiki/Pallava_dynasty "Pallava dynasty"), [Chalukya dynasty](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty"), [Pandya dynasty](/wiki/Pandya_dynasty "Pandya dynasty"), and [Rashtrakuta dynasty](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_dynasty "Rashtrakuta dynasty")

The ancient Chera empire collapsed due to the continuous invasions carried out by the [Kalabhras](/wiki/Kalabhra_dynasty "Kalabhra dynasty"), the [Pallavas](/wiki/Pallava_dynasty "Pallava dynasty"), the [Chalukyas](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty"), the [Pandyas](/wiki/Pandya_dynasty "Pandya dynasty"), and the [Rashtrakutas](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_dynasty "Rashtrakuta dynasty") during the period between 500 CE and 800 CE for nearly three centuries.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) This era marked the migration of [Nambudiri](/wiki/Nambudiri "Nambudiri") [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") into the district, which later led to a transformation in the social structure of the region after the 10th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The ancient prominent religions in the district like [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") began to vanish after the 10th century CE. [Sri Mulavasam](/wiki/Sri_Mulavasam "Sri Mulavasam") was a prominent centre of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in the [Indian peninsula](/wiki/Indian_peninsula "Indian peninsula") until the early medieval period, prior to the Chera-Chola wars of the 11th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

### Medieval history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Medieval history")]

The regions included in the modern-day district of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") is described well in the medieval Kerala literature which includes works written in both [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") and [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The 14th century [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") work *Sukasandesam* describes about [Kaduthuruthy](/wiki/Kaduthuruthy "Kaduthuruthy").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Similarly the 17th century Sanskrit work *Bhramara Sandesam* contains details about [Kumaranalloor](/wiki/Kumaranalloor "Kumaranalloor") and [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The 18th century Sanskrit literary work *Chathaka Sandesam* also contains references to [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

*[Unnuneeli Sandesam](/wiki/Unnuneeli_Sandesam "Unnuneeli Sandesam")*, a work which belongs to the [Malayalam literature](/wiki/Malayalam_literature "Malayalam literature") written during early decades of the 14th century CE, contains a short geographical and political description of the regions and the medieval [feudal states](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") that lied between [Kaduthuruthy](/wiki/Kaduthuruthy "Kaduthuruthy") and [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) It contains details about the medieval kingdoms of [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") (1102–1749) and [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") (1102–1750) those together constitute the modern district of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The writings and the records of the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") as well as the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Malabar "Dutch Malabar"), who visited and interfered in the political affairs of the district after the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") (1498 CE), such as the *[Hortus Malabaricus](/wiki/Hortus_Malabaricus "Hortus Malabaricus")*, contain significant information about the geographical and the political conditions of the region during the late medieval period.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

#### The Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram (800 – 1102 CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram (800 – 1102 CE)")]

Main article: [Chera Perumals of Makotai](/wiki/Chera_Perumals_of_Makotai "Chera Perumals of Makotai")

See also: [Vazhappally copper plate](/wiki/Vazhappally_copper_plate "Vazhappally copper plate")

See also: [Medieval Cholas](/wiki/Medieval_Cholas "Medieval Cholas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/South_India_in_early_11th_century_AD.jpg/250px-South_India_in_early_11th_century_AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_India_in_early_11th_century_AD.jpg)

[Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") during 11th century CE (the region which is marked as *Chera*)

The area included in the modern-day district of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") was part of three *Nadus* (provinces) during the period of the [Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram](/wiki/Chera_Perumals_of_Makotai "Chera Perumals of Makotai") (800–1102 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era")) for nearly three centuries.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The region around the city of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") was known as *Munjunadu*, while the [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom")-[Meenachil](/wiki/Meenachil "Meenachil") region, which lies to the north of *Munjunadu* was included in a province called *Vempolinadu* (known as *Bimbali Desam* in Sanskrit).[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The third province was the *Nantuzhainadu* which constitutes the modern-day [Taluks](/wiki/List_of_taluks_of_Kerala "List of taluks of Kerala") of [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery") and [Thiruvalla](/wiki/Thiruvalla "Thiruvalla").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) This period saw the decline of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), which were once prominent in the region, along with the growth of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") characterised by a large-scale migration of the [Nambudiri](/wiki/Nambudiri "Nambudiri") [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") into the region.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The territory of the Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram disintegrated into several small [feudal states](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") by early 12th century CE as a result of the continuous war that occurred between the Chera Perumals and the [Medieval Cholas](/wiki/Medieval_Cholas "Medieval Cholas") throughout the 11th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Vazhappally_copper_plate_%289th_century_AD%29.jpg/250px-Vazhappally_copper_plate_%289th_century_AD%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vazhappally_copper_plate_(9th_century_AD).jpg)

The [Vazhappally copper plate](/wiki/Vazhappally_copper_plate "Vazhappally copper plate") (882/883 CE) written in [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam"), found near [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The [Vazhappally copper plate](/wiki/Vazhappally_copper_plate "Vazhappally copper plate") (c. 882/83 CE[[8]](#cite_note-:28-8)) is a copper plate inscription written in [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") language that dates back to the 9th century CE found at [Vazhappally](/wiki/Vazhappally "Vazhappally") in Kottayam district.[[9]](#cite_note-:12-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:02-10) Recent scholarship puts the date of the plate in c. 882/83 CE.[[11]](#cite_note-:3-11) The inscription is engraved on a single copper plate (with five lines on both sides) in an early form of the [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") language in [Vatteluttu](/wiki/Vatteluttu "Vatteluttu") script with some [Grantha characters](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script"). The contents of the plate are incomplete.[[9]](#cite_note-:12-9) The inscription was discovered by V. Srinivasa Sastri from *Thalamana Illam* near [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery").[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12) The plate is owned by *Muvidathu Madom*, [Thiruvalla](/wiki/Thiruvalla "Thiruvalla").[[9]](#cite_note-:12-9) The record is dated to the twelfth [regnal year](/wiki/Regnal_year "Regnal year") of Chera Perumal king [Rama Rajasekhara](/wiki/Rajashekhara_(Chera_king) "Rajashekhara (Chera king)") (882/83 CE).[[11]](#cite_note-:3-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Perunna_inscription_of_Rama_Kulasekhara_%281099_AD%29.jpg/250px-Perunna_inscription_of_Rama_Kulasekhara_%281099_AD%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perunna_inscription_of_Rama_Kulasekhara_(1099_AD).jpg)

The [Perunna](/wiki/Perunna "Perunna") inscription (1099 CE) of [Rama Kulasekhara](/wiki/Rama_Kulasekhara "Rama Kulasekhara") written in [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam"), found near [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery").[[13]](#cite_note-:9-13)

Inscriptions related to [Rama Kulasekhara](/wiki/Rama_Kulasekhara "Rama Kulasekhara") (1089–1123 CE) of Chera Perumal dynasty can be found at [Perunna](/wiki/Perunna "Perunna") near [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery").[[14]](#cite_note-:35-14) The [Perunna](/wiki/Perunna "Perunna") inscription dates back to the year 1099 CE (the 10th regional year of Rama Kulasekhara).[[13]](#cite_note-:9-13)

#### Thekkumkur dynasty (1102–1749 CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Thekkumkur dynasty (1102–1749 CE)")]

Main article: [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur")

See also: [Battle of Changanassery](/wiki/Battle_of_Changanassery "Battle of Changanassery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Map_of_Thekkumkur_in_1790.jpg/250px-Map_of_Thekkumkur_in_1790.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Thekkumkur_in_1790.jpg)

A political map of the Kingdom of [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") (1102–1749)

The province *Vempolinadu* (*Bimbali Desam*) of the Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram attained political autonomy in 1102 CE following the Chera-Chola wars of the 11th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The *Vempolinadu* royal family got evolved into two independent branches by the 12th century CE – the southern branch among them later came to be known as the kingdom of [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) It included the modern-day [Taluks](/wiki/List_of_taluks_of_Kerala "List of taluks of Kerala") of [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [Kanjirappally](/wiki/Kanjirappally "Kanjirappally"), [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), and [Thiruvalla](/wiki/Thiruvalla "Thiruvalla").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The kings of the Thekkumkur dynasty are described by the titles *Bimbaleeshas* and *Manikandas* in the Sanskrit literary works.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) [Vennimala](/wiki/Vennimala "Vennimala") and [Manikandapuram](/wiki/Manikandapuram "Manikandapuram") were the early headquarters of the Thekkumkur dynasty.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Later it was transferred to [Thazhathangady](/wiki/Thazhathangady_Juma_Mosque "Thazhathangady Juma Mosque") near the town of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Thazhathangady is one of the places in Kerala where a church, a mosque, and a Hindu temple co-exist together, which points out the religious harmony that existed under the *Thekkumkur Rajas*. At times, the town of [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery") acted as headquarters of the Thekkumkur dynasty.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The *Thekkumkur Rajas* were vassals to the [Kingdom of Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") until the mid-18th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Thekkumkur Rajas also allied with the [Kingdom of Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") and the Kingdom of [Odanad](/wiki/Odanad "Odanad") ([Kayamkulam](/wiki/Kayamkulam "Kayamkulam")) against the military invasions of the kingdom of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") into its northern kingdoms that occurred during the 1740s.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) As a result, Anizham Thirunal [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_Varma "Marthanda Varma"), the architect of the modern Travancore, attacked and annexed the kingdom of [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") into Travancore in 1749 during the [Battle of Changanassery](/wiki/Battle_of_Changanassery "Battle of Changanassery"), after his annexation of [Kayamkulam](/wiki/Kayamkulam "Kayamkulam") in 1746.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The Thekkumkur king took refuge in the kingdom of the [Zamorin](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") of Calicut following his defeat in the battle.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

#### Vadakkumkur dynasty (1102–1750 CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Vadakkumkur dynasty (1102–1750 CE)")]

Main article: [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur")

[Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") was the northern branch of the erstwhile province of *Vempolinadu* which attained political autonomy in 1102 CE after the collapse of the Chera Perumals of Mahodayapuram.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The rulers of this dynasty were also known by the titles *Bimbaleeshas* and *Manikandas* in the contemporary Sanskrit works just like their [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") counterparts. The kingdom of [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") included the modern-day Taluks of [Ettumanoor](/wiki/Ettumanoor "Ettumanoor") and [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom") along with a portion of the [Meenachil](/wiki/Meenachil "Meenachil") Taluk.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) [Kaduthuruthy](/wiki/Kaduthuruthy "Kaduthuruthy") was the earlier headquarters of the [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") dynasty which was later transferred into [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Vadakkumkur was a vassal state of the [Kingdom of Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") until 1750.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") explorers described [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") as "The Pepper country" due to the availability and high scale production of good quality pepper there during the 16th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The [Dutch Malabar](/wiki/Dutch_Malabar "Dutch Malabar") who became an influential power in the territory of Vadakkumkur during the 17th century CE and the first half of the 18th century CE had a trade centre at [Vechoor](/wiki/Vechoor "Vechoor") (in [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom") [taluk](/wiki/List_of_taluks_of_Kerala "List of taluks of Kerala")) in Vadakkumkur.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The Kingdom of *Kizhmalanadu* (1102–1600), which had included the modern-day Taluks of [Muvattupuzha](/wiki/Muvattupuzha "Muvattupuzha") and [Thodupuzha](/wiki/Thodupuzha "Thodupuzha") with its headquarters at Karikode near Thodupuzha, merged with the kingdom of [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") around 1600 CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

[Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") Raja was also a part of the combined military alliance formed by the kingdoms of [Odanad](/wiki/Odanad "Odanad") (Kayamkulam), [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur"), and [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") against the expansion of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") into the northern territories.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) As a result, Anizham Thirunal [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_Varma "Marthanda Varma"), the architect of the modern Travancore, attacked and annexed Vadakkumkur in 1750.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The Vadakkumkur Raja sought asylum in the kingdom of the [Zamorin](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") of Calicut.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Later he returned back into Travancore.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

#### Poonjar dynasty (1160–1750 CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Poonjar dynasty (1160–1750 CE)")]

Main article: [Poonjar dynasty](/wiki/Poonjar_dynasty "Poonjar dynasty")

In 1157 CE, [Kulothunga Chola](/wiki/Kulothunga_I "Kulothunga I"), who belonged to the lineage of the [Medieval Cholas](/wiki/Medieval_Cholas "Medieval Cholas"), entered into a war with Manavikrama Kulasekhara Perumal, who belonged to the [Pandya dynasty](/wiki/Pandya_dynasty "Pandya dynasty") of [Madurai](/wiki/Madurai "Madurai").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Upon the failure in the battle, Manavikrama appointed his brother Maravarman Sreevallabha as the monarch of the [Pandya dynasty](/wiki/Pandya_dynasty "Pandya dynasty") and left Madurai with his family and some of his trusted servants.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Manavikrama crossed the [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats") and sought political asylum in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Manavikrama purchased a vast tract of land bordering the Pandyan territories, from the ruler of [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") which comprised the [Meenachil](/wiki/Meenachil "Meenachil") taluk the regions of [Poonjar](/wiki/Poonjar "Poonjar"), Erattupeta, Pala, Patthanamthitta, Thodupuzha, etc. along with the [High Range](/wiki/High_Range,_Kerala "High Range, Kerala") region of modern-day [Idukki district](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district") in 1160 CE, which originally belonged to the [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") Rajas.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) This incident commenced the beginning of the [Poonjar dynasty](/wiki/Poonjar_dynasty "Poonjar dynasty"). They were a sovereign state.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Though disputed, the territory of Poonjar was claimed by the Kingdom of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") in 1899 AD. Travancore claimed that the sovereignty reverted to them following the annexation of the principalities of [Thekkumkur](/wiki/Thekkumkur "Thekkumkur") and [Vadakkumkur](/wiki/Vadakkumkur "Vadakkumkur") in 1749–1750.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The land ownership was not disputed.

The [Kannan Devan Hills](/wiki/Kannan_Devan_Hills "Kannan Devan Hills") on the [High Range](/wiki/High_Range,_Kerala "High Range, Kerala") region were given on lease on 11 July 1877 by the ruler of the [Poonjar dynasty](/wiki/Poonjar_dynasty "Poonjar dynasty") to John Daniel Munroe from London and for [tea plantations](/wiki/History_of_tea_in_India "History of tea in India"). The land and the plantations were later resumed by the [Government of Kerala](/wiki/Government_of_Kerala "Government of Kerala") through the Kannan Devan Hills (Resumption Of Lands) Act, 1971.[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, the act only addressed the issue of sovereignty which was taken over by the State of Kerala with the accession of Travancore to the Union of India. The ownership of the Kannan Devan plantation (125000 acres)leased to John Monroe, and subsequently transferred to Tata Finlay, and finally to Tata Tea was not relinquished by the Poonjar royal family. This was established by a court challenge to the status, which was resolved by a Kerala High Court order in the early 1990s. The order was in favor of the Poonjar royal family establishing ownership. The lease terms are currently being fulfilled with regards to lease payments as per the original deed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Progress and Social Reformation during the Travancore era (1750–1947)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Progress and Social Reformation during the Travancore era (1750–1947)")]

Main article: [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore")

See also: [Kerala reformation movement](/wiki/Kerala_reformation_movement "Kerala reformation movement") and [Vaikom Satyagraha](/wiki/Vaikom_Satyagraha "Vaikom Satyagraha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Cms_college_old_building.JPG/250px-Cms_college_old_building.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cms_college_old_building.JPG)

The [CMS College Kottayam](/wiki/CMS_College_Kottayam "CMS College Kottayam"), established in 1815, was the first [Western model](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") institute of higher education in India.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Travancore_1871.JPG/250px-Travancore_1871.JPG)](/wiki/File:Travancore_1871.JPG)

A map of the [British](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") [princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") published in 1871

Entire portion of the modern-day district of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") became part of the erstwhile [British](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") [princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") by 1750 CE. This era marked considerable progress and social reformation in all spheres.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society") press at [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") was established in 1821 by Rev. [Benjamin Baily](/wiki/Benjamin_Bailey_(missionary) "Benjamin Bailey (missionary)"), a British missionary, as the first printing press in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[16]](#cite_note-16) They had also established the [CMS College Kottayam](/wiki/CMS_College_Kottayam "CMS College Kottayam") in 1815 as the first [Western style](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") institute of higher education in India. The region progressed much in its literacy rate by the end of the 19th century CE.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) It became one of the most literate regions in the [British Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") by 1860.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Due to its remarkable progress in the fields of literacy, education, and printing, many early newspapers and magazines in [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") such as *[Deepika](/wiki/Deepika_(newspaper) "Deepika (newspaper)")* (1887) and *[Malayala Manorama](/wiki/Malayala_Manorama "Malayala Manorama")* (1888) were established in [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam").[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

The same period also saw the commencement of [Kerala reformation movement](/wiki/Kerala_reformation_movement "Kerala reformation movement") with an aim to eradicate the [Untouchability](/wiki/Untouchability "Untouchability"), inequality, and the irrational practices existed in the Kerala society for many centuries which ultimately led to the movements like the [Vaikom Satyagraha](/wiki/Vaikom_Satyagraha "Vaikom Satyagraha") (1924).[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) Kottayam has been involved in a number of political movements, including the 'Malayali Memorial' movement.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The goal of the movement was to seek more representation for [Malayalis](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") in the [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") civil service irrespective of their caste and religion.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)

[Vaikom Satyagraha](/wiki/Vaikom_Satyagraha "Vaikom Satyagraha"), a protest against [caste discrimination](/wiki/Caste_system_in_India "Caste system in India"), took place in Kottayam district in 1924.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7) The district also participated in the protests for responsible government in Travancore, which ended with the overthrow of [Sir C. P. Ramaswami Iyer](/wiki/Sir_C._P._Ramaswami_Iyer "Sir C. P. Ramaswami Iyer"), who was the [Divan](/wiki/Divan "Divan") of the British princely state of Travancore in 1947.[[7]](#cite_note-feudal_states_in_early_18th_century_Kerala-7)

During the [Travancorean](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") administration, the [British](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") [princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of Travancore was divided into four revenue divisions- the [Northern division](/wiki/Northern_Division_(Travancore) "Northern Division (Travancore)") headquartered at [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), the [Central division](/wiki/Central_Division_(Travancore) "Central Division (Travancore)") headquartered at [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam"), the [Southern division](/wiki/Southern_Division_(Travancore) "Southern Division (Travancore)") headquartered at [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram"), and the [High Range division](/wiki/High_Range,_Kerala "High Range, Kerala") with its headquarters at [Devikulam](/wiki/Devikulam "Devikulam").[[18]](#cite_note-1931_census-18)

[Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") was the headquarters of the northernmost revenue division in Travancore which included the [Taluks](/wiki/List_of_taluks_of_Kerala "List of taluks of Kerala") of [North Paravur](/wiki/North_Paravur "North Paravur"), [Kunnathunad](/wiki/Kunnathunad "Kunnathunad"), [Muvattupuzha](/wiki/Muvattupuzha "Muvattupuzha"), [Thodupuzha](/wiki/Thodupuzha "Thodupuzha"), [Meenachil](/wiki/Meenachil "Meenachil"), [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom"), and [Cherthala](/wiki/Cherthala "Cherthala") in 1931.[[18]](#cite_note-1931_census-18)

### Post-independence (1947–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Post-independence (1947–present)")]

See also: [Indian Independence Act 1947](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947") and [Instrument of Accession](/wiki/Instrument_of_Accession "Instrument of Accession")

At the time of the integration of the state of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") and [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") into the state of [Travancore-Cochin](/wiki/Travancore-Cochin "Travancore-Cochin") in 1949 following the integration of the [British](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") [Princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") and [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") into the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India"), the erstwhile revenue divisions were reorganised into [districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala") and the [Divan](/wiki/Divan "Divan") Peshkars gave way to [district collectors](/wiki/District_collector "District collector"), paving the way for the birth of the Kottayam district on 1 July 1949.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The erstwhile [Kingdom of Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") was reorganized into [Thrissur district](/wiki/Thrissur_district "Thrissur district") and Taluks of [North Paravur](/wiki/North_Paravur "North Paravur") and [Kunnathunad](/wiki/Kunnathunad "Kunnathunad") were transferred to that district.[[20]](#cite_note-1951c-20) Similarly the [Cherthala](/wiki/Cherthala "Cherthala") Taluk was transferred into the newly formed [Kollam district](/wiki/Kollam_district "Kollam district") on 1 July 1949.[[20]](#cite_note-1951c-20) The [High Range](/wiki/High_Range,_Kerala "High Range, Kerala") division of the erstwhile Travancore was merged with the remaining Taluks of the erstwhile Kottayam division to form the Kottayam district as a part of the inauguration of the new Indian state of [Travancore-Cochin](/wiki/Travancore-Cochin "Travancore-Cochin") on 1 July 1949.[[20]](#cite_note-1951c-20) The new district of Kottayam at that time contained eight Taluks – Changanassery, Kottayam, Vaikom, Meenachil, Muvattupuzha, Thodupuzha, Devikulam, and Peerumede.[[20]](#cite_note-1951c-20)

As a part of the formation of the new Indian state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") according to the [States Reorganisation Act, 1956](/wiki/States_Reorganisation_Act,_1956 "States Reorganisation Act, 1956"), many jurisdictional changes were done in the district.[[21]](#cite_note-1961c-21) Two new Taluks, namely [Kanjirappally](/wiki/Kanjirappally "Kanjirappally") (from Changanassery and Thiruvalla) and [Udumbanchola](/wiki/Udumbanchola "Udumbanchola") (from Devikulam and Peerumede) were carved out on 1 October 1956.[[21]](#cite_note-1961c-21) After a month, a new Taluk called [Kuttanad](/wiki/Kuttanad "Kuttanad") was carved out from Changanassery and [Ambalappuzha](/wiki/Ambalappuzha "Ambalappuzha").[[21]](#cite_note-1961c-21) The Kuttanad Taluk was transferred into the newly formed [Alappuzha district](/wiki/Alappuzha_district "Alappuzha district") on 17 August 1957. The Taluks of Thodupuzha and Muvattupuzha were transferred into the newly formed [Ernakulam district](/wiki/Ernakulam_district "Ernakulam district") on 1 April 1958.[[21]](#cite_note-1961c-21)

On 26 January 1972, the three [High Range](/wiki/High_Range,_Kerala "High Range, Kerala") Taluks, namely Devikulam, Udumbanchola, and Peerumede, along with [Thodupuzha](/wiki/Thodupuzha "Thodupuzha") from the [Ernakulam district](/wiki/Ernakulam_district "Ernakulam district") were separated from Kottayam district to form a new district called [Idukki](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district").[[22]](#cite_note-1981c-22) The district of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") took its current shape on 26 January 1972. Now it contains five Taluks- [Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery"), [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom"), [Meenachil](/wiki/Meenachil "Meenachil"), and [Kanjirappally](/wiki/Kanjirappally "Kanjirappally").

The municipality of [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam") was declared as the first town in India to achieve 100% literacy in 1989.[[23]](#cite_note-niti-23) The Multidimensional Poverty Index prepared by [NITI Aayog](/wiki/NITI_Aayog "NITI Aayog") based on the National Family Health Survey 2015–16 declared Kottayam as the first [Indian district](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_India "List of districts of India") to achieve zero multidimensional poverty rate.[[23]](#cite_note-niti-23)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Climate")]

There are no distinct seasons in Kottayam, as it has a tropical climate like that of the rest of Kerala. Humidity is high and rises to about 90% during the rainy season. Kottayam gets rain from two [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") seasons, the south-west monsoon and the north-east monsoon, and accumulates an average rainfall of around 3600 mm per year. The south-west monsoon starts in June and ends in September, and the north-east monsoon season is from October to November. Pre-monsoon rains from March to May are accompanied by thunder and lightning; the highest rainfall during this period in Kerala is received in Kottayam. December, January, and February are cooler, while March, April, and May are warmer. The highest temperature recorded in Kottayam was 38.5 °C on 6 April 1998, and the lowest was 15 °C on 13 December 2000.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Kottayam district experienced intense [red rainfall](/wiki/Red_rain_in_Kerala "Red rain in Kerala") in 2001, during which the rain was colored red, yellow, green, and black.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Tourism and wildlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tourism and wildlife")]

Kottayam has a network of rivers, [backwaters](/wiki/Backwater_(river) "Backwater (river)"), ancient religious places, and hill stations. Local tourist places include:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/KumarakomHouseBoat.jpg/250px-KumarakomHouseBoat.jpg)](/wiki/File:KumarakomHouseBoat.jpg)

House Boat in [Kumarakom](/wiki/Kumarakom "Kumarakom")

* [Kumarakom](/wiki/Kumarakom "Kumarakom"), a tourist destination located near the city of Kottayam. It contains the [Kumarakom Bird Sanctuary](/wiki/Kumarakom_Bird_Sanctuary "Kumarakom Bird Sanctuary"), which is known for migratory birds.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* [Vembanad Lake](/wiki/Vembanad_Lake "Vembanad Lake") is home to traditional cargo boats called [Kettuvallams](/wiki/Kettuvallams "Kettuvallams"), which are modified into cruise boats and houseboats.[[27]](#cite_note-kottayam.nic.in-27) [Pathiramanal](/wiki/Pathiramanal "Pathiramanal") is a small island located in the Vembanad Lake, and is only accessible by boat.[[27]](#cite_note-kottayam.nic.in-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Ilaveezhapoonchira.jpg/250px-Ilaveezhapoonchira.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilaveezhapoonchira.jpg)

[Ilaveezha Poonchira](/wiki/Ilaveezha_Poonchira "Ilaveezha Poonchira")

* [Ilaveezha Poonchira](/wiki/Ilaveezha_Poonchira "Ilaveezha Poonchira") is a [hill station](/wiki/Hill_station "Hill station") in Kottayam.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* [Vagamon](/wiki/Vagamon "Vagamon") is a hill station in Kerala. It is located primarily in the [Idukki district](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district"), but includes the [Meenachil](/wiki/Meenachil "Meenachil") taluk and [Kanjirappally](/wiki/Kanjirappally "Kanjirappally") taluk as well.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* During the [Onam](/wiki/Onam "Onam") festival in August and September, water boat races are conducted in Kottayam. The major one is the [Nehru Trophy Boat Race](/wiki/Nehru_Trophy_Boat_Race "Nehru Trophy Boat Race"). The Thazhathangadi boat race in [Kummanam](/wiki/Kummanam "Kummanam") is over a century old.
* [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom"), a historical town.
* [Illickal Kallu](/wiki/Illickal_Kallu "Illickal Kallu"), the highest point in the Kottayam district.

Malarikkal and Panachikkad in Kottayam is famous for Ambal fest.

Kottayam is linked by major roads and rail to other prominent cities in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"), and also by waterways allowing for waterborne travel. The Kottayam Kumali, Ettumanoor-Ernakulam, Kottayam-Pathanamthitta, Thiruvalla-Kidangoor Central Kerala Bypass, and MC road are the major roads in the district. The nearest airport is the [Cochin International Airport](/wiki/Cochin_International_Airport "Cochin International Airport"). SWTD operates ferry services from different parts of the Kottayam district. The ferry service from Vaikom to Thavanakkadavu in the Alappuzha district is the longest. India's first solar ferry service boat, 'Adhithya', operates from Vaikom.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

Kottayam has a mountainous terrain as well as low-lying areas very close to sea level. Depending on the location, different varieties of food and cash crops are cultivated. Rice is the principal crop extensively cultivated in low-lying regions like [Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom") and [Upper Kuttanad](/wiki/Upper_Kuttanad "Upper Kuttanad"). The district occupies third place in the production of rice in Kerala behind [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad_district "Palakkad district") and [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha_district "Alappuzha district").

Although it is the staple food of the people, the area under cultivation is dwindling due to more lucrative cash crops such as rubber plantations for which Kottayam significantly contributes to the overall rubber production in India. Kottayam is India's largest rubber producer. Rubber trees provide a stable income for farmers and the climate is ideal for rubber plantations. Though the highlands are more suitable, cultivation has spread to almost all regions. Other crops cultivated include tapiocas, coconuts, peppers, and vegetables. To enhance rubber productivity, the government of India has set up a [Rubber Board](/wiki/Rubber_Board "Rubber Board") as well as a rubber research institute in Kottayam.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Industry")]

| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(August 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The district lacks refineries, ports, and an airport, which are needed for major industries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]Aside from two public sector companies, [Hindustan Newsprint](/wiki/Hindustan_Newsprint "Hindustan Newsprint") at Velloor and Travancore Cements at Nattakom, industries in the district consist mostly of small and medium-size operations. These mainly include the publishing and processing of rubber or [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex"), and manufacturing of rubber-based products.

Confined mostly to the Vaikom area of the district is an industry of [coir](/wiki/Coir "Coir") processing and making coir products.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Consisting of more than twenty [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative"), it employs around 20,000 people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the hand-loom sector, eight cooperative societies employ 2,100 persons. The district's forests include varieties of [softwood](/wiki/Softwood "Softwood") and other varieties of timber providing the raw material for several small enterprises in the production of [plywood](/wiki/Plywood "Plywood"), packing cases, splints, [veneers](/wiki/Wood_veneer "Wood veneer"), and furniture.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first printing press in Kerala, [C.M.S. Press](/wiki/C.M.S._Press "C.M.S. Press"), was established in 1821 by Rev. Benjamin Bailey, a British missionary. Malayalam-English and English-Malayalam dictionaries were published in Kottayam in 1846 and 1847. The only cooperative society of writers, authors and publishers (SPCS) for publishing books and periodicals was established in 1945. Kottayam is home to a number of books and periodicals, and is the center of publishing business in the state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Publishing houses like [Malayala Manorama](/wiki/Malayala_Manorama "Malayala Manorama"), [Mathrubhumi](/wiki/Mathrubhumi "Mathrubhumi") publications, [Labour India Publications](/wiki/Labour_India_Publications "Labour India Publications") Ltd, [Mangalam Publications](/wiki/Mangalam_Publications "Mangalam Publications"), [Deepika](/wiki/Deepika_(newspaper) "Deepika (newspaper)"), [D. C. Books](/wiki/D._C._Books "D. C. Books"), V Publishers, Vidhyamitram, *[Kerala Kaumudi](/wiki/Kerala_Kaumudi "Kerala Kaumudi")* daily and Kerala Kaumudi Flash are also publishers in the district. The city of Kottayam hosts several book exhibitions every year.

According to the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_census_of_India "2011 census of India") Kottayam district has a [population](/wiki/Demographics_of_India "Demographics of India") of 1,974,551,[[39]](#cite_note-districtcensus-39) roughly equal to the nation of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia")[[40]](#cite_note-cia-40) or the US state of [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico").[[41]](#cite_note-41) This gives it a ranking of 234th in India (out of a total of [640](/wiki/Districts_of_India "Districts of India")).[[39]](#cite_note-districtcensus-39) The district has a population density of 896 inhabitants per square kilometre (2,320/sq mi).[[39]](#cite_note-districtcensus-39) Its [population growth rate](/wiki/Family_planning_in_India "Family planning in India") over the decade 2001–11 was 1.32%.[[39]](#cite_note-districtcensus-39) Kottayam has a [sex ratio](/wiki/Human_sex_ratio "Human sex ratio") of 1040 [females](/wiki/Women_in_India "Women in India") for every 1000 males, and a [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_in_India "Literacy in India") of 97.21%, the highest in the state and 4th highest in India. 28.63% of the population lives in urban areas. Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes make up 7.79% and 1.11% of the population respectively.[[39]](#cite_note-districtcensus-39)

[Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") is the predominant language, spoken by 98.88% of the population. [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") is spoken by a small minority in urban areas.[[42]](#cite_note-languages-42)

According to the 2011 Indian Census, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Kerala "Hinduism in Kerala") (49.81%) is the largest religion in Kottayam, with a significant [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Kerala "Christianity in Kerala") minority (43.48%). Syro-Malabar, Orthodox, Jacobites, CSI and Pentecostals are the main Christian sects. The Muslim population constitutes 6.41% of the district.[[43]](#cite_note-religion-43)

The three major religious communities in the Kottayam district are [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), Christianity, and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). The [Nair Service Society](/wiki/Nair_Service_Society "Nair Service Society")'s headquarters are located in the district's town of [Perunna](/wiki/Perunna "Perunna"), Changanassery. The Mannam memorial, created in memory of social reformer [Mannathu Padmanabha Pillai](/wiki/Mannathu_Padmanabha_Pillai "Mannathu Padmanabha Pillai"), is also located there.

The headquarters of the [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church") (Malankara Church/Indian Orthodox Church), is located in [Devalokam](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church#Catholicate_Palace "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church"), [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"). It is the official headquarters of the [Malankara Metropolitan](/wiki/Malankara_Metropolitan "Malankara Metropolitan") and the [Catholicos](/wiki/Catholicos "Catholicos") of the East.

The Madhya Kerala Diocese of the [Church of South India](/wiki/Church_of_South_India "Church of South India") (CSI) has its headquarters in Kottayam.

The [Knanaya](/wiki/Knanaya "Knanaya") Christian community (both the Catholic and Jacobite factions) also has its headquarters in Kottayam district.

The [Thazhathangady Juma Mosque](/wiki/Thazhathangady_Juma_Mosque "Thazhathangady Juma Mosque"), which is one of the oldest [*Masjids*](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") in the country, is also located at Kottayam.

[Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"), [Thiruvalla](/wiki/Thiruvalla "Thiruvalla"), and [Chengannur](/wiki/Chengannur "Chengannur") are the railway stations for pilgrims heading to the Hindu holy site of [Sabarimala](/wiki/Sabarimala "Sabarimala").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kottayam_district&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Kottayam district](/wiki/Education_in_Kottayam_district "Education in Kottayam district")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Cms_college1.JPG/250px-Cms_college1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cms_college1.JPG)

[CMS College Kottayam](/wiki/CMS_College_Kottayam "CMS College Kottayam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Sbcollegechry.jpg/250px-Sbcollegechry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sbcollegechry.jpg)

[SB College Changanassery](/wiki/St._Berchmans_College "St. Berchmans College")

In the 17th century, a Dutch school was started at Kottayam, which was short-lived. The first English school in Kerala, and the first college in India, was established in 1817 by the [Church Missionary Society](/wiki/Church_Missionary_Society "Church Missionary Society") of England under the leadership of [Col. John Munro](/wiki/John_Munro,_9th_of_Teaninich "John Munro, 9th of Teaninich"), as [CMS College](/wiki/CMS_College_Kottayam "CMS College Kottayam"). Later in 1891, the [clergies](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") of [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), under the leadership of [Fr Charles Lavigne](/wiki/Charles_Lavigne "Charles Lavigne"), established [St Berchmans English High School, Changanassery](/wiki/St._Berchmans_Higher_Secondary_School "St. Berchmans Higher Secondary School"), one of the first residential English High School in Central Travancore. On seeing a need for instituition for higher education in [Kottayam - Malabar region](/wiki/Northern_Division_(Travancore) "Northern Division (Travancore)"), under the leadership of [Venerable Mar Thomas Kurialacherry](/wiki/Thomas_Kurialacherry "Thomas Kurialacherry"), Bishop of [Archeparchy of Changanassery](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Archeparchy_of_Changanacherry "Syro-Malabar Catholic Archeparchy of Changanacherry") with help of [Syro Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church"), established the famous [SB College](/wiki/St._Berchmans_College "St. Berchmans College") in [Changanasserry](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery") in 1922. Today CMS College Kottayam and SB College Changanasserry stands as a landmark and has made a huge contribution to education in the district. Thus, Kottayam became India's first district with 100% literacy in 1989.

Notable educational institutions in the district include:-

* [SB College Changanassery](/wiki/St._Berchmans_College "St. Berchmans College")
* [CMS College Kottayam](/wiki/CMS_College_Kottayam "CMS College Kottayam")
* [Government College, Kottayam](/w/index.php?title=Government_College,_Kottayam&action=edit&redlink=1 "Government College, Kottayam (page does not exist)")[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* [Government Medical College, Kottayam](/wiki/Government_Medical_College,_Kottayam "Government Medical College, Kottayam")
* [Indian Institute of Information Technology, Kottayam](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Information_Technology,_Kottayam "Indian Institute of Information Technology, Kottayam")(IIIT-K)
* [Indian Institute of Mass Communication](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Mass_Communication "Indian Institute of Mass Communication"), Kottayam (IIMC-K)
* [K. R. Narayanan National Institute of Visual Science and Arts](/wiki/K._R._Narayanan_National_Institute_of_Visual_Science_and_Arts "K. R. Narayanan National Institute of Visual Science and Arts")
* Kottayam Institute of Technology & Science
* [Mahatma Gandhi University, Kerala](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi_University,_Kerala "Mahatma Gandhi University, Kerala").
* [Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Technology, Kottayam](/wiki/Rajiv_Gandhi_Institute_of_Technology,_Kottayam "Rajiv Gandhi Institute of Technology, Kottayam") (Government Engineering College)
